sets.q: What is the momentum of a 10 kg object moving at 5 m/s?
A 4 kg ball moves at 8 m/s. What is its momentum?
A 15 kg cart rolls at 3 m/s. Find its momentum.
A 0.2 kg tennis ball moves at 25 m/s. What is its momentum?
A 60 kg person runs at 4 m/s. What is the momentum?
A force of 10 N acts on an object for 3 seconds. What is the impulse?
A force of 25 N acts for 4 seconds. What is the impulse?
A 50 N force is applied for 2 seconds. What impulse is delivered?
A net force of 8 N acts on a ball for 5 seconds. What is the impulse?
A braking force of 600 N acts for 3 seconds. What impulse is applied?
A 2 kg object at rest is hit with an impulse of 16 N·s. What is its final velocity?
sets.given: Impulse-Momentum Theorem: J = m × (vf − vi), vi = 0

sets.q: A 5 kg object at rest receives an impulse of 30 N·s. What is the final velocity?
A 4 kg object at rest receives impulse 20 N·s. What is the final speed?
A 10 kg object at rest receives impulse 50 N·s. What is the final velocity?
A 3 kg ball at rest is struck with impulse 12 N·s. What is the final speed?
Which has greater momentum: 2 kg at 10 m/s or 5 kg at 3 m/s?
Which has greater momentum: a 3 kg ball at 8 m/s or a 6 kg ball at 5 m/s?
Which has greater momentum: a 0.5 kg baseball at 40 m/s or a 10 kg bowling ball at 2 m/s?
Which has greater momentum: a 1 500 kg car at 10 m/s or a 1 000 kg car at 20 m/s?
A 0.1 kg bullet at 400 m/s vs a 50 kg person at 1 m/s. Which has greater momentum?
A 6 kg object moves from 4 m/s to 10 m/s. What impulse caused this change?
sets.given: Impulse = change in momentum: J = m × (vf − vi)

sets.q: A 3 kg ball accelerates from 2 m/s to 8 m/s. What was the impulse?
A 5 kg object changes velocity from 1 m/s to 7 m/s. Find the impulse.
A 2 kg ball changes speed from 5 m/s to 15 m/s. What impulse was applied?
A 10 kg cart speeds up from 0 m/s to 6 m/s. What impulse acted on it?
A 1 500 kg car moves at 20 m/s. What is its momentum?
A 2 000 kg truck moves at 15 m/s. What is the momentum?
A 900 kg car moves at 25 m/s. What is the momentum?
A 1 200 kg vehicle moves at 18 m/s. Find the momentum.
A 5 000 kg bus moves at 12 m/s. What is its momentum?
In a closed system with no external forces, what is true about total momentum?
sets.given: This is one of the most fundamental laws in physics.
opts: Total momentum before = Total momentum after
Momentum is only conserved in elastic collisions

sets.q: The Law of Conservation of Momentum states:
sets.given: A 'closed system' means no external forces act.
opts: Total momentum of a closed system is constant

sets.q: Before a collision, two objects have a combined momentum of 80 kg·m/s. After they stick together, the total momentum is:
sets.given: Conservation of momentum: total momentum is conserved.

sets.q: Two ice skaters push each other apart from rest. Their total momentum after the push is:
sets.given: They start at rest, so total initial momentum = 0.
opts: Twice the mass of one skater

sets.q: Which of the following is a consequence of conservation of momentum?
sets.given: Consider what happens when a gun fires a bullet.
opts: A gun recoils backward when a bullet is fired forward
A moving object always stops when it hits a wall
Heavier objects always have more momentum
Momentum is always converted to energy

sets.q: A 3 kg ball at 10 m/s collides with a 7 kg stationary ball and they stick together. What is the combined velocity?
A 5 kg trolley at 6 m/s collides and sticks to a 10 kg trolley at rest. Find the combined velocity.
A 2 kg cart at 8 m/s collides and sticks to a 6 kg cart at rest. Find the final velocity.
A 4 kg ball at 5 m/s sticks to a 6 kg ball at rest. What is the final velocity?
A 8 kg object at 3 m/s collides and sticks with a 4 kg stationary object. Find the final velocity.
Why does a longer contact time reduce the force of an impact (e.g., an airbag)?
sets.given: Impulse: J = F × t = Δp. If Δp is fixed, what happens to F as t increases?
opts: F decreases because F = Δp / t — same impulse over more time
F increases because more time means more force
F is unchanged — only momentum changes
F increases because the object decelerates faster

sets.q: A padding material extends the collision time from 0.01 s to 0.1 s. If the impulse is fixed, what happens to the average force?
sets.given: J = F × t → F = J / t
opts: Force decreases by a factor of 10
Force increases by a factor of 10

sets.q: Why do gymnasts bend their knees when landing?
sets.given: Bending knees increases contact time during landing.
opts: To increase contact time and reduce peak force
To increase the force of impact

sets.q: A boxer 'rolls with a punch' to reduce injury. This works because:
sets.given: Rolling with the punch increases the time of contact.
opts: Longer contact time → smaller average force (same impulse)
It reduces the mass of the fist

sets.q: Car crumple zones are designed to:
sets.given: J = F × t. Crumple zones increase t during a crash.
opts: Increase collision time to reduce peak force
Decrease collision time to reduce force

sets.q: A 5 kg object at 4 m/s right collides head-on with a 5 kg object at 4 m/s left. They stick. Final velocity?
sets.given: Right = positive, left = negative. m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)vf

sets.q: Object A: 3 kg at 6 m/s right. Object B: 3 kg at 6 m/s left. They collide and stick. Final velocity?
sets.given: Equal and opposite momenta — they cancel.

sets.q: Two identical 2 kg balls move toward each other at 5 m/s each. They collide and stick. Final velocity?
sets.given: Equal and opposite momenta in a head-on collision.

sets.q: Object A: 10 kg at 4 m/s right. Object B: 10 kg at 4 m/s left. They collide and stick. Final velocity?
A 6 kg ball at 3 m/s east and a 6 kg ball at 3 m/s west collide and stick. What is the final velocity?
sets.given: Equal mass, equal speed, opposite directions.
